title: Discovering the Human Body: How Pioneers of Medicine Solved the Mysteries of the Body's Structure and Function
abstract:
and Health Administration regula¬
tions for precaution are not included.
Occupational Medicine is a valuable
book for
the allergist, pulmonary
physician,
or
occupational
health
worker. It does organize under sepa¬
rate chapters useful data relating the
relationship of environmental expo¬
sure to asthma.
Mark J. Ellenbogen, MD
Cambridge, Mass
Cardiology
The Practice of Cardiology, edited by Robert
Arnold Johnson, Edgar Haber,
and
W.
Gerald
Austen, 1,179 pp, with illus, $59.95, Boston, Little
Brown & Co, 1980.
Clinical cardiology has undergone
dramatic changes in the past decade,
and a text that deals with the current
state of the science and art of cardiol-
ogy is long overdue. The Practice of
Cardiology is a highly readable, clini-
cally oriented series of 42 essays by 52
contributors (cardiologists, thoracic
surgeons, anesthesiologists, and psy-
chiatrists) from the Massachusetts
General Hospital. The three editors
have organized the book into nine
sections that cover four major areas:
syndromes common to cardiac disease
in general, specific cardiac diseases,
diagnostic techniques, and the periop-
erative
care of the cardiac patient.
Each chapter begins with
a brief
outline of the major topics
to be
covered and concludes with a perti-
nent list of references in alphabetic
order by first author. The numerous
illustrations
are generally of good
quality.
The authors and editors are to be
congratulated for emphasizing and
highlighting
the
clinical judgment
and decision-making process that the
experienced physician uses in the care
of the patient with a cardiac disorder.
For example, in the section that deals
with the evaluation and management
of patients with angina pectoris, the
rational selection of special test pro¬
cedures, such
as the exercise ECG,
stress radioisotope imaging, ambula¬
tory monitoring, and coronary left
ventricular angiography,
is lucidly
presented. These procedures are ini¬
tially discussed in the context of the
diagnostic and prognostic assessment
of the patient with chest pain, and the
details of these procedures
are de¬
scribed in separate sections.
As with any book that is drawn
from the experiences of a single insti¬
tution,
a certain amount of provin¬
cialism is evident. The chapter
on
echocardiography is almost exclusive¬
ly devoted to the older M-mode tech¬
nique, and the
more powerful two-
dimensional
sector
scanner
and
phased-array
methods
are
hardly
touched on. These minor inadequacies
are more than offset by the compre¬
hensive and thorough approach
to
patient care that extends throughout
this practical and authoritative text.
The authors and editors of this
book have made innumerable contri¬
butions to the cardiological literature,
and it is refreshing to have the clini¬
cal and academic experience of these
physicians blended together in
one
unified
text. Students,
house staff,
and practicing physicians involved in
the
care
of patients with
cardiac
problems will find The Practice of
Cardiology invaluable; it provides a
current gold standard for the practice
of cardiology.
Arthur J. Moss, MD
University of Rochester
School of Medicine
Rochester, NY
Childhood Cancer
Status of the Curability of Childhood Can-
cers, edited by Jan van Eys and Margaret
P.
Sullivan
(University
of
Texas
System
Cancer
Center,
M.
D.
Anderson Hospital
and
Tumor
Institute 24th Annual Conference), 329 pp, with
illus, $31.50, New York, Raven Press, 1980.
This
monograph
represents
the
published proceedings of a two-day
symposium at
a foremost pediatric
oncology center on the curability of
childhood cancer. Pediatric oncology
has been the pacesetter in develop-
ments in combination and multimo-
dality therapy, resulting in impres-
sive cure rates. The monograph traces
this phenomenon over the last decade
and predicts that in another decade
one of 1,000 persons reaching age 20
years will have survived cancer: an
epidemic of cancer survivors! In pedi-
atrics, metastatic disease is no longer
uniformly fatal. Questions
on
the
quality of that survival, including
physical
and psychological
aspects
and the potential for second malig-
nancy, are addressed throughout the
monograph.
The first of four principal sections
covers
the biologic
and functional
definitions of cure. The second section
traces the spectacular success of sol-
id-tumor therapy in terms of inte-
grated chemotherapy, surgical
ad-
vances, and radiation therapy along
with extensive supportive modalities;
the third section similarly covers the
hematologic malignancies.
Each chapter represents a provoca-
tive analysis of where we are now and
where we are headed. One of pédiatrie
oncology's most significant achieve¬
ments is that we are now working
toward refining therapy to lessen the
side effects and long-term sequelae
while achieving the same cure rate.
Success is so good that the alleviation
and prevention of significant sequelae
is itself an area of research. Active¬
ly addressed is the question of the
extent to which conventional therapy,
including surgery and chemotherapy,
can be reduced and remain as effec¬
tive.
The fourth section, "The Future
Beyond Cure," is exceptionally stimu¬
lating; it examines the concept of
living
vs "existing but cured." The
estimated 12% incidence of a second
malignancy in cured patients hangs
over children's heads. Can we learn
anything
about
cancer
prevention
from this extremely high-risk group?
The psychosocial price of cure is high
and
includes
repeated hospitaliza-
tions,
poor school attendance,
and
lowered expectations. Cured but ill-
prepared adults cannot take their just
places in society.
The monograph is intended for the
pédiatrie hematology-oncology team
in its
broadest
sense,
as well
as
trainees in pédiatrie oncology. It pro¬
vides a succinct and complete analysis
of our spectacular success during the
last decade and poses the provocative
questions that we must solve in the
next decade.
Ruth Andrea Seeler, MD
Cook County Hospital
Chicago
History
Discovering the Human Body: How Pioneers
of Medicine Solved the Mysteries of the Body's
Structure and Function, by Bernard Knight, 192
pp. with illus, $17.95,
New York, Lippincott
&
Crowell, 1980.
"Medical history,"
as the distin-
guished historian Henry Sigerist once
wrote, "teaches
us where
we
come
from, where we stand in medicine at
the
present
time,
and
in
what
direction
we
are marching." Many
have
documented
this
fascinating
journey, usually in
a biographical
form,
or by historical
sequence
or
social context. Dr Knight approaches
it as a sleuth, discovering "How pio-
neers of medicine solved the mys-
teries of the body's structure and
function." His skills
as
a forensic
pathologist and author (in addition to
textbooks, he has published historical
fiction and six detective novels) are
well suited to the task. In less than
200 fluently written, lavishly illus-
trated, and amply indexed pages, he
has given us a unique account of how
the human body was "discovered."
The format is
a system-oriented
tour of medical history, showing how
discoveries
were
made
and
after
Downloaded From: http://jama.jamanetwork.com/ by a Osaka University User  on 06/03/2015
 whom they were named. The histories
of
anatomy
and
physiology
are
treated more broadly, with interest-
ing asides into anatomic etymology.
Most fascinating is the book's exposi¬
tion of eponymy:
"Such
names
as
Wirsung's
duct
can conjure
up
a
memory of a Bavarian doctor assassi¬
nated in Padua in the seventeenth
century, an image which the sombre
pancreatic
duct
can
never
create.
Similarly,
the
premaxillary
bone
hardly stirs the emotions, but the
knowledge that it is also named after
Goethe, Germany's greatest poet—
who fought a duel at ten—must sure¬
ly inject some interest into all but the
most unimaginative
of minds."
A
final chapter on the history of human
dissection (entitled "Corpus-Delecti")
was particularly delectable in detail¬
ing this exciting story.
Although Discovering the Human
Body has neither the scope of Major's
History of Medicine, nor the depth of
Garrison's classic text, it is an enter¬
taining and informative compilation
of medical names and deeds. As such
it should prove a welcome addition to
the libraries of all those interested in
the subject, physicians and laymen
alike.
Irwin M. Siegel, MD
Chicago
Rheumatology
Rehabilitation
Management
of
Rheumatic
Conditions, edited by George E. Ehrlich (Rehabili-
tation Medicine Library, John V. Basmajian, ed),
322 pp, 63 illus, $34.95, Baltimore, Williams &
Wilkins Co, 1980.
The mission of this book appears to
be
to advocate that patients with
long-term
rheumatic
diseases
be
treated by a multidisciplinary team.
The 18 contributors, of whom four are
physicians, address many topics, but
without giving enough specific infor-
mation to guide the physician in the
treatment of his
own patient. For
example,
"the
patient
should
be
instructed as to what is the normal
range (of motion)
and
how it
is
obtained." However, no exercises are
described,
nor is there
a table of
normal
ranges
of
motion.
"Warm
moist heat in its various forms (moist
heating pads,
hot packs,
paraffin
dips) can be effective," but no illus-
trations or recommendations regard-
ing duration or temperature are pro-
vided. There is a well-written chapter
on splinting by an occupational thera-
pist, but it is limited to treatment of
the hand and wrist. Nothing further
is said about the treatment of con-
tractures. Self-help devices
are not
mentioned.
The most informative chapter, by
S. H. Roth on drug therapy, presents
a concise up-to-date reference on the
pharmacology and toxicity of most of
the drugs used in treating rheumatic
diseases. Some unexpected topics are
presented, such as recommendations
about
how
to organize
a disease-
related community agency, including
fund-raising techniques; and how
a
truck may be modified for the conve-
nience of
an arthritic driver. The
reader who is curious about the delib-
erations that led to the enactment of
the Federal Rehabilitation Act will
find
them
excerpted.
My
favorite
among the platitudes is the podia-
trist's revelation that "the foot is the
structural foundation of erect ambu-
lation."
The approaches to patient care that
are represented
are laudable,
and
many are just as pertinent to patients
who have chronic nonrheumatic ail-
ments.n Unfortunately,
neither
the
leadership of the advocated team nor
the audience to whom this volume is
addressed is defined.
Thomas G. Benedek, MD
Veterans Administration
Hospital
Pittsburgh
Coagulation
Prevention of Venous Thrombosis and Pul-
monary Embolism, by J.
G. Sharnoff,
135 pp,
$25, Boston, GK Hall, 1980.
My initial impression in reviewing
J. G. Sharnoff's Prevention of Venous
Thrombosis and Pulmonary Embo-
lism
was that it
was
a somewhat
evangelical attempt by the author to
excite the heparin-thromboembolism
establishment into a mystical realiza-
tion
that
there
is
a
difference
between small-dose (his) and low-
dose (theirs) heparin prophylaxis.
Then I spent a morning reviewing
references (the monograph
is well
referenced)
and
my
skepticism
abated. The author, beginning in 1956,
took
a piece
of pathological data
(increased
pulmonary
megakaryo-
cytes)
and rudimentary laboratory
data
(increased platelets
and
de-
creased Lee-White clotting times in
postoperative patients) and developed
a theory (postoperative hypercoagula-
ble state secondary to thrombocytosis
related to stress of surgery and exac-
erbated by mobilization)
that
ex-
plained to him the high incidence of
postoperative morbidity and mortali-
ty related to thromboembolic disease.
His theory led him to the institution
of a trial of prophylactic hepariniza-
tion in which he strove to prevent
hypercoagulable
or
hypocoagulable
states from occurring preoperatively,
intraoperatively, and postoperatively
as defined by a simple bedside clot¬
ting time (the modified
Dale
and
Laidlow coagulometer).
By 1962, he
was presenting data
demonstrating efficacy and safety in
a small-dose subcutaneous heparin
regimen.
In
1970,
he presented
a
series
in
Lancet
of
750 high-risk
patients treated with small-dose hep¬
arin, showing significantly decreased
mortality compared with 18,000 un¬
treated average-risk patients. It was
only at this point, after a decade of
his own elegant experience, that oth¬
ers began to report the efficacy of
unmonitored
low-dose
prophylactic
heparin administration.
The author's experience and
suc¬
cess with his small-dose heparin regi¬
men with bedside clotting time moni¬
toring, form the crux of the mono¬
graph and should not be ignored. This
is especially so, since this therapy
promises little thromboembolic dis¬
ease and little hemorrhage, even in
high-risk situations such as hip sur¬
gery. However, it falls one step short
of the author's enthusiastic sugges¬
tion that his regimen "be the method
against which all other prophylactic
methods for the prevention of throm¬
boembolic
disease
are
measured."
This step, of course, is a well-designed
clinical study comparing the safety
and efficacy of low-dose heparin pro¬
phylaxis with the author's small-dose
heparin prophylaxis
in preventing
clinically significant thromboembolic
disease.
When the text strays from prophy¬
laxis to other aspects of thromboem¬
bolic disease, the work is less imag¬
inative and less authoritative. The
author, for example, recommends the
same subcutaneous regimen for the
treatment of active deep-vein throm¬
bosis and pulmonary embolism as he
does for prophylaxis, and cites unpub¬
lished data, leading me again to ques¬
tion evangelism.
Nonetheless, based on the data pre¬
sented, I am swayed that the author's
approach to heparin prophylaxis has
merit. Beginning therapy at the onset
of
immobilization
and
monitoring
such therapy with a simple reproduc¬
ible test that allows adjustment of
therapy has great appeal to me. I look
forward to a clinical comparison of
small- and low-dose heparin and I am
anxious to gain experience with moni¬
toring
subcutaneous
prophylactic
heparin with the author's modified
Dale and Laidlow coagulometer.
T. Michael White MD
University of Connecticut Health Center
School of Medicine
Farmington
Bradley Memorial Hospital
Southington, Conn
